event: Samson Destroying the Temple of Dagon
symbols: two pillars — the structural supports of pagan power, toppled by God's instrument of judgment
Samson between the columns — self-sacrificial strength, a type of Christ who conquered through his own death
collapsing temple — divine judgment on idolatry, the overthrow of false gods
falling Philistines — the destruction of God's enemies through the weakness made strong by grace
circular composition — the totality of destruction, the completeness of divine judgment
description: A circular engraving depicting the dramatic moment of the temple's collapse. At center, the muscular figure of Samson pushes apart two massive stone columns, his body straining with superhuman effort. All around him, the temple of Dagon crumbles: stone blocks, beams, and architectural fragments cascade downward. Philistine men and women tumble from upper galleries, their bodies contorted in terror and agony. In the background through an archway, a crowd watches the catastrophe unfold. The composition is densely packed with figures and architectural debris, conveying total devastation.
theological_description: Samson's destruction of the Philistine temple (Judges 16:23-30) is the climactic act of Israel's most paradoxical judge. Blinded, humiliated, and enslaved, Samson prays one final time: 'O Lord God, remember me and strengthen me only this once' (Judg 16:28). In this moment of utter dependence on God, Samson achieves his greatest victory -- killing more Philistines in his death than in his entire life. The Church Fathers universally recognized Samson as a type of Christ. As Samson stretched his arms between two pillars and died to destroy his enemies, so Christ stretched His arms on the cross and died to destroy sin, death, and the devil. Augustine emphasized that Samson's voluntary death for the deliverance of his people parallels Christ's voluntary self-offering: 'No one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord' (John 10:18). The destruction of Dagon's temple prefigures the overthrow of all idolatry and false worship that the gospel accomplishes. Samson's blindness at the moment of his greatest victory also speaks to the paradox of 2 Corinthians 12:9-10: 'when I am weak, then I am strong' -- divine power perfected in human weakness.

scripture_refs.reason: The direct narrative: Samson, blinded and captive, pushes apart the pillars of Dagon's temple, killing himself and thousands of Philistines.
Samson's final prayer for strength, his ultimate act of dependence on God.
Samson listed among the heroes of faith who through faith conquered kingdoms and were made strong out of weakness.
Christ's voluntary laying down of His life, paralleled by Samson's self-chosen death for the deliverance of his people.
Christ disarming the principalities and powers and triumphing over them through the cross, as Samson triumphed through his death.
Power perfected in weakness -- blind, captive Samson at his moment of greatest divine empowerment.
The weakness of God is stronger than men -- Samson's humiliation becomes God's instrument of devastating judgment.

_meta.title: Samson Destroying the Temple of the Philistines
